1: Psalm 98 opens without a superscription (only mizmôr — psalm) and parallels Ps 96 closely. שִׁירוּ לַיהוָה שִׁיר חָדָשׁ כִּי-נִפְלָאוֹת עָשָׂה (shîrû layhwāh shîr ḥādāsh kî-nipĕlāʾôt ʿāśāh, "Sing to the LORD a new song, for he has done wondrous things!") — the shîr ḥādāsh (new song) of Ps 96:1 returns, but this time with a reason: kî nipĕlāʾôt ʿāśāh (because he has done wonders). The new song is new because the deed it celebrates is new — a fresh act of divine power that old songs could not yet express. הוֹשִׁיעָה-לּוֹ יְמִינוֹ וּזְרוֹעַ קָדְשׁוֹ (hôshîʿāh-llô yĕmînô ûzĕrôaʿ qōdshô, "His right hand and his holy arm have won him the victory!") — yāmîn: the right hand (the hand of power and swearing-of-oaths — Isa 41:10); zĕrôaʿ qōdshô: his holy arm (Isa 52:10: "the LORD has bared his holy arm before the eyes of all the nations, and all the ends of the earth shall see the salvation of our God"). The arm and hand are warrior-body imagery for divine rescue accomplished alone.
2: הוֹדִיעַ יְהוָה יְשׁוּעָתוֹ לְעֵינֵי הַגּוֹיִם גִּלָּה צִדְקָתוֹ (hôdîaʿ yĕhwāh yĕshûʿātô lĕʿênê haggôyim gillāh ṣidqātô, "The LORD has made his salvation known; he has revealed his righteousness before the nations!") — hôdîaʿ (hiphil: caused to know, made known — a public announcement of what was previously unknown). Yĕshûʿāh: salvation (the noun whose root gives the name Joshua/Jesus). Gillāh ṣidqātô: he has uncovered/revealed his righteousness (as a garment is pulled aside to show what was underneath — Isa 53:1; Paul cites Isa 52:15 in Rom 15:21 for the gospel going to the gentiles). The salvation is both accomplished and publicly displayed.
3: זָכַר חַסְדּוֹ וֶאֱמוּנָתוֹ לְבֵית יִשְׂרָאֵל (zākar ḥasdô wĕʾĕmûnātô lĕbêt yiśrāʾêl, "He has remembered his steadfast love and his faithfulness to the house of Israel!") — zākar: to remember with active effect (not merely mental recall but the divine act of bringing something out of suspension — Gen 8:1: "God remembered Noah"; Exod 2:24: "God remembered his covenant with Abraham"). Ḥesed wĕʾĕmûnāh: the covenant pair — steadfast love (the bond of loyalty) and faithfulness (dependable truth). רָאוּ כָל-אַפְסֵי-אָרֶץ אֵת יְשׁוּעַת אֱלֹהֵינוּ (rāʾû kol-ʾapsê-ʾāreṣ ʾêt yĕshûaʿ ʾĕlōhênû, "All the ends of the earth have seen the salvation of our God!") — ʾapsê-ʾāreṣ: the ends/extremities of the earth (the uttermost limits — Isa 40:28; 45:22: "turn to me and be saved, all the ends of the earth").
4: הָרִיעוּ לַיהוָה כָּל-הָאָרֶץ פִּצְחוּ וְרַנְּנוּ וְזַמֵּרוּ (hārîʿû layhwāh kol-hāʾāreṣ pitsĕḥû wĕrannĕnû wĕzammêrû, "Shout for joy to the LORD, all the earth! Break forth and sing and make music!") — rûaʿ (hiphil: to shout/blast the war-cry of joy); pātsaḥ: to burst out (in song — Isa 14:7; 52:9; 54:1; 55:12: the bursting joy of the redeemed creation); rānan: to ring out; zāmar: to make music with instrument. Four verbs in cascade — the gathering intensity of a crescendo that universal salvation demands.
5: זַמְּרוּ לַיהוָה בְּכִנּוֹר בְּכִנּוֹר וְקוֹל זִמְרָה (zammĕrû layhwāh bĕkhinnôr bĕkhinnôr wĕqôl zimrāh, "Sing praises to the LORD with the harp, with the harp and the sound of melody!") — kinnôr: the lyre (David's instrument — 1 Sam 16:16,23; Ps 33:2; 144:9). The repetition of "with the harp, with the harp" is emphatic insistence: not a perfunctory accompaniment but the full-throated dedication of the instrument's capacities. Qôl zimrāh: the voice of melody/song (the human voice joining the instrument).
6: בַּחֲצֹצְרוֹת וְקוֹל שׁוֹפָר הָרִיעוּ לִפְנֵי הַמֶּלֶךְ יְהוָה (baḥăṣōṣĕrôt wĕqôl shôpār hārîʿû lipnê hammelek yĕhwāh, "With trumpets and the blast of the ram's horn shout for joy before the King, the LORD!") — ḥăṣōṣĕrôt: the long metal trumpets (silver trumpets made at Moses's command — Num 10:1-10: to be blown at feasts, new moons, and before battles); shôpār: the ram's horn (blown at Sinai — Exod 19:16,19; Lev 25:9 for the Jubilee; Zech 9:14 for the final battle). The two instruments together cover both the sacral (trumpet) and the ancient convocation signal (ram's horn). Hammelek YHWH: before the King YHWH — the shout is a royal acclamation before the enthronement (Ps 97:1).
7: יִרְעַם הַיָּם וּמְלֹאוֹ תֵּבֵל וְיֹשְׁבֵי בָהּ (yirʿam hayyām ûmĕlōʾô têbêl wĕyōshĕbê bāh, "Let the sea thunder with all that fills it, the world and all who dwell in it!") — as in Ps 96:11, the sea is commanded to join the acclamation. Rāʿam: to thunder, roar (the word for the sea's most powerful voice — Job 37:2,5; Ps 77:18; 104:7). The sea's roar which was once the symbol of chaos threatening creation (Ps 93:3-4; 98:7) is here recruited into the royal acclamation of the God who stilled the chaos.
8: נְהָרוֹת יִמְחֲאוּ-כָף יַחַד הָרִים יְרַנֵּנוּ (nĕhārôt yimḥăʾû-khāp yaḥad hārîm yĕrannênnû, "Let the rivers clap their hands; let the mountains sing together for joy!") — nĕhārôt yimḥăʾû-khāp: "rivers clap their hands" — a uniquely striking image (rivers have no hands — the metaphor extends personification into the absurd to capture the magnitude of the joy). Māḥāʾ kap: to clap the palm (the gesture of acclaim and joy — 2 Kgs 11:12; Ps 47:1). The mountains singing (yĕrannênnû: ring out, shout) creates a duet: rivers clapping, mountains singing — all creation in full-throated worship.
9: לִפְנֵי-יְהוָה כִּי בָא לִשְׁפֹּט הָאָרֶץ יִשְׁפֹּט-תֵּבֵל בְּצֶדֶק וְעַמִּים בְּמֵישָׁרִים (lipnê-yĕhwāh kî bāʾ lishpōṭ hāʾāreṣ yishpōṭ-têbêl bĕṣedeq wĕʿammîm bĕmêshārîm, "before the LORD, for he is coming to judge the earth! He will rule the world in righteousness and the peoples with fairness!") — almost word-for-word Ps 96:13, forming a deliberate doublet: the enthronement cluster of Psalms 93-99 keeps returning to the coming Judge as its goal and ground. Mêshārîm: straightness/equity (from yāshar: straight). The world song of vv7-8 (seas, rivers, mountains) all points here: the judge who comes is the one before whom all creation properly worships.
